topic: Someone learns their favorite author plagiarized their stories
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. A polite, sterile notification from the publisher, informing me that a recent collection I adored had been found to contain significant, uncredited material lifted directly from my unpublished manuscript. My hands, gripping the mouse so tightly my knuckles were white, trembled slightly as I reread the attached comparison. Every sentence, every carefully crafted scene, was there, transposed, polished, and now, belonging to someone else. A slow, cold burn started low in my stomach and spread, tightening into a hard knot behind my ribs. I stared at the screen, the blue light reflecting in my eyes, and felt a surge of hot, furious energy. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet apartment. I needed air. I needed to scream.

[story 2]

"This is absurd." The words were barely a whisper, but the force behind them shook the small study. I paced the length of the worn Persian rug, my breath coming in ragged, shallow gasps. I stopped abruptly, spinning around to face the empty bookshelves, my jaw clenched so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. It wasn't just a slight misstep; it was a calculated theft, a blatant appropriation of my soul poured onto another person's page. A deep, simmering resentment rose up, hot and suffocating. I walked over to the window, staring out at the indifferent city lights, my shoulders rigid. My fists clenched at my sides, the tension radiating off me like heat. I wanted to shatter something, anything, to release the pressure building inside.

[story 3]

I read the letter twice, then a third time, forcing myself to remain outwardly calm. But inside, the quiet composure was fracturing. My vision narrowed, the edges of the room blurring as a profound sense of betrayal washed over me. It felt like someone had taken the very blueprint of my inner world and built a replica, claiming the original as their own. A tremor ran through my hands as I set the paper down on the desk. I leaned back in my chair, the leather groaning under the sudden shift in weight. I let out a long, slow exhale, but it wasn't relief; it was the sound of something breaking under unbearable strain. My gaze swept over the room, sharp and accusatory, as if daring anyone to challenge the audacity of the deception.
